narrative: Dear Alex, I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring so much into your law school dreams, but let me walk you through the story the admissions model saw in your profile, like piecing together chapters of a book that didn't quite add up to the ending we hoped for. Your Year 3 grades placed you in the bottom decile at your school—a 1.0 ranking—which stood out as the strongest signal pulling toward bar exam struggles, painting a picture of challenges that persisted right up to the end of your undergrad journey. Layered on top was your Year 1 decile of 2.0, reinforcing that early hurdles in academics set a tough tone from the start. Your LSAT score of 38 followed closely as another key factor, suggesting the intense reasoning demands of law might amplify those patterns. Even with your upper-middle family income offering some stability, it somehow wove into the narrative as tilting toward difficulty, perhaps in how it intersected with other pressures. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 added to this storyline, showing a consistent thread of grades that the model linked to bar passage risks. As a white male going full-time, those details nudged the outlook further in that direction, completing a profile where every major piece seemed to align toward a 58.8% chance of not passing the bar. It's not that any one thing defined you—it's this combination that made the model cautious, like a coach spotting patterns in a player's stats before the big game. The good news? You're target_law 1.0 all the way, and with targeted steps like bolstering those deciles through stronger showings or LSAT prep, you can rewrite this story for the next chapter. Hang in there—this is just one plot twist.
